comments.text: I believe the stronger version is false. For every $n$, let $p_1p_2\cdots p_{m-1}$ be the largest primorial less than $n$, and let $k=n-p_1p_2\cdots p_{m-1}<p_1p_2\cdots p_m$. Then, $\omega(n-k)=m-1$, and $\log k<\vartheta(p_m)<m\left(\log m+\log\log m-1+o(1)\right)$, where $\vartheta$ is the Chebyshev function, so $\frac{\log k}{\log\log k}<\frac{\vartheta(p_m)}{\log\vartheta(p_m)}<\frac{m\left(\log m+\log\log m-1+o(1)\right)}{\log m+\log\log m+(1+o(1))\frac{\log\log m}{\log m}}<m-C$ for any constant $C$ for large $m$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Nice! Interesting that it actually needs the third term $p_n = n (\log n + \log\log n - 1 + o(1))$ in the asymptotic expansion for the n^th prime (which by the way is not yet within reach of the PNT+ Lean project). A back of the envelope calculation suggests than an upper bound of $\frac{\log k}{\log\log k} + O( \frac{\log k}{(\log\log k)^2})$ might still be possible, though.

Aristotle has formalized the implication from this asymptotic expansion. EDIT: I noticed that the way Aristotle used the asymptotic seemed weakenable to the asymptotic already proved in PNT+, so I swapped it out and re-ran Aristotle on the one lemma that used it, and it still went through.

Nice! I was sure that one needed both $\vartheta(x) = x + o(x / \log x)$ and $p_n = n \log n + n \log\log n - n + o(n)$, both of which require a medium strength version of PNT, but indeed just knowing $p_n = (1+o(1)) n \log n$ (weak PNT) is enough because this gives $\log p_n = \log n + \log\log n + o(1)$ which is enough to give $\vartheta(p_n) = n \log n + n \log\log n - n + o(n)$ (which is what is needed here) even without the two stronger asymptotics for $\vartheta$ and $p_n$. So I can honestly say I learned something from Aristotle; a minor thing to be sure, but still useful. (This formalization was previously recorded in Section 6 of the wiki, but I have moved it to Section 3 because of this.)
